text: This video shows our technique of robotic restorative proctocoloctomy in a patient with ulcerative colitis.
The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg with the operating table tilted fifteen degrees to the left after the placement of trocars.
The robotic arms are mounted onto the trocars, and robotic instruments are inserted into the surgical field following the completion of targeting by the robotic system.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle by gently retracting the ileocolic junction anteriorly and laterally, revealing the anatomical structures for further dissection.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum overlying the ileocolic pedicle, isolating the pedicle from surrounding tissue using robotic instruments.
The surgeon clips and transects the ileocolic artery and vein using the high ligation technique, ensuring clear separation of these vascular structures.
The surgeon performs mesenteric dissection using a medial to lateral approach, carefully navigating between the embryological planes anterior to Toldt's fascia, Gerota's fascia, and the duodenum.
The surgeon performs a medial dissection of the hepatic flexure, separating it from surrounding tissues using robotic instruments.
The surgeon extends the dissection up to the root of the middle colic vessels, continuing the surgical approach along the identified anatomical planes.
The surgeon clips and transects the middle colic artery and vein, completing the vascular control in this region.
The surgeon applies traction to the transverse colon, entering the bursa omentalis and dividing the gastrocolic tissue from right to left, extending up to the splenic flexure.
The robotic arms facilitate the redirection of the dissection to the right lower quadrant, allowing precise manipulation and access to multiple quadrants during the procedure.
The surgeon applies medial traction to separate the lateral attachments of the right colon, visibly detaching it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon prepares the terminal ileum for transection, positioning it for the next surgical step.
The surgeon uses an endoscopic linear stapler to transect the ileum, completing the separation of the terminal ileum from the adjacent structures.
The robotic arms rotate from the right side to the left side of the patient, repositioning for the dissection of the left colon and rectum.
The operating table is tilted fifteen degrees to the right, and the robotic arms are repositioned to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon retracts the small bowel loops out of the pelvis and positions them in the right upper quadrant. Robotic instruments are then inserted into the surgical field.
The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon anteriorly, incises the peritoneum at the promontorium level, and enters the aorta mesenteric window.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric artery pedicle and carefully dissects it, ensuring the preservation of the surrounding nerve plexus.
The surgeon clips and transects the inferior mesenteric artery using robotic instruments, completing the dissection of this vascular structure.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric vein using robotic instruments, following the same technique as used for the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon dissects the mesentery of the left colon in a medial-to-lateral fashion, carefully preserving the left ureter, ornatal vessels, and autonomic nerves.
The surgeon retracts the left colon medially and divides its lateral attachments, completing the dissection of the colon from surrounding structures.
The surgeon takes down the splenic flexure, detaching it from its surrounding structures.
Following the completion of the colonic dissection, the robotic instruments are repositioned to focus on the pelvic region, preparing for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon performs a dissection of the rectum following the principles of total mesorectal excision, carefully separating the rectal tissue from surrounding structures.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectum by incising the lateral peritoneal reflection below the sacral promontory, then frees it posteriorly from the sacral hollow, separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection in the plane between the fascia propria recti and the presacral fascia, carefully separating these layers.
The surgeon begins by dissecting the posterior mesorectum, allowing for easier handling of the mesorectum and facilitating subsequent lateral dissection.
The surgeon mobilizes the right lateral side of the mesorectum, continuing the dissection within the pelvic region using robotic assistance.
The surgeon mobilizes the left side of the mesorectum, continuing the dissection process to free the rectum from surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs dissection on the anterior plane of the mesorectum after completing bilateral dissection, carefully separating the mesorectum from the anterior structures.
The surgeon continues the mesorectal dissection, progressing towards the anorectal ring, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon adjusts the angle of the robotic camera from a downward to an upward position to visualize the intersynthetic area.
The levator ani muscles are visible in the surgical field, along with the surrounding eye muscles.
The surgeon transects the rectum using an articulating endoscopic linear stapler, which is introduced through the suprapubic trocars.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through an extended suprapubic incision, originating from the suprapubic trochar site.
The surgeon constructs a J-pouch using a linear stapler, forming the pouch in the typical manner.
The surgeon places the anvil of the circular stapler into the distal end of the J-pouch, preparing for the next step in the colorectal procedure.
Following the re-establishment of pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon introduces the circular staple trans-anally, preparing for anastomosis within the colorectal region.
The surgeon performs the pouch anal anastomosis, connecting the constructed J-pouch to the anal canal.
The surgeon carefully aligns the intestine to prevent twisting and ensures the staple line does not interrupt the vagina during the procedure.
The surgeon creates a diverting loop ileostomy, marking the completion of the operation.
